does: This paper introduces CE-Bench, a contrastive evaluation benchmark designed to measure the interpretability of sparse autoencoders applied to large language models. The benchmark uses a curated dataset of contrastive story pairs where each pair contains narratives that activate different sets of features, allowing the method to assess whether SAE-extracted features genuinely correspond to interpretable concepts. The evaluation is lightweight and does not require an external LLM judge, unlike many existing SAE evaluation approaches. The authors validate CE-Bench through comprehensive studies demonstrating that it achieves over 70% Spearman correlation with SAEBench results while being more efficient and accessible.
connects: This work builds directly on recent efforts to develop sparse autoencoders as mechanistic interpretability tools for understanding LLM internals, responding to a recognized need for scalable and reliable evaluation methods in this space. Prior work like SAEBench established automated evaluation approaches but often relied on expensive external LLM judges for assessment. The paper connects to broader interpretability research seeking to decompose neural network activations into human-understandable features, extending methodology from explainability and feature importance studies. It engages with contrastive learning frameworks that have proven effective in representation learning, adapting these principles to the specific problem of validating SAE feature quality.
enables: CE-Bench enables more efficient large-scale evaluation and iteration on sparse autoencoder designs by removing dependency on expensive external LLM evaluations, potentially accelerating research in mechanistic interpretability. The lightweight nature of the benchmark supports development of better SAE architectures and training procedures by providing faster feedback loops for practitioners. This work points toward future directions in building complementary evaluation suites for interpretability tools and could facilitate wider adoption of SAEs in safety-critical applications where understanding model behavior is essential. The open-sourced benchmark and dataset enable community-driven improvements and serve as infrastructure for developing more sophisticated interpretability evaluation methods.
